critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully designed with temporal splits, frozen preprocessing, and reasonable baseline equivalence. Most obvious leakage paths are addressed. However, I identify three genuine methodological issues worth scrutinizing:

**Issue 1: BERT embedding trained to predict analyst disposition is a latent label-leakage risk at the incident level**

The claim: The BERT model is fine-tuned on training-set tickets to predict analyst disposition, and its embeddings are used as features. Since preprocessing is fit only on months 1–12, this appears safe.

Why it might be wrong: Incident response tickets are written *after* triage is complete. They contain analyst conclusions, IOC summaries, and remediation steps — not just the raw alert metadata present at the moment of triage. Fine-tuning BERT on these post-resolution texts to predict disposition, and then using those embeddings as input features, effectively smuggles the analyst's conclusion back into the input signal. At test time, if the ticket text used to build embeddings is the *resolved* ticket (not the pre-resolution stub), the model is trained to extract the exact signal (analyst judgment) it is supposed to learn to predict. Even if the ticket timestamps are after the alert timestamp (as the temporal constraint specifies), the *content* of ticket text at resolution time is different from the content at triage time. The design does not clarify whether embeddings are computed from ticket text as it existed at alert arrival or from the final resolved ticket. If the latter, this is a direct label-leakage mechanism through the text feature.

Evidence one way or the other: Compare model performance when the BERT embedding is removed entirely vs. included. If MRR@10 drops sharply without it, audit what the embedding is encoding — ablate by replacing ticket text with alert-title-only text (which is genuinely available at triage time). A clean methodology would restrict ticket text to alert-submission text or pre-triage notes only, or exclude ticket text from the feature set entirely.

**Issue 2: Incident clustering via ticket assignment creates a circular dependency between label construction and feature space**

The claim: Incidents are defined by analyst ticket assignment (all alerts resolved in the same ticket are one incident). This is used both to construct the ground-truth relevance labels and to define the listwise ranking groups.

Why it might be wrong: Ticket clustering reflects analyst behavior during the *training period*. Analysts may cluster alerts differently based on familiarity, workload, or tool quirks — and this clustering behavior may be systematically correlated with alert features (e.g., analysts may always group alerts from a specific rule into one ticket, inflating apparent co-relevance). More critically: if the ranking model is evaluated at the incident level where incidents are defined by the analyst's own grouping decisions, then part of what the model must implicitly learn is the analyst's grouping heuristics, not just the true-positive signal. On the test set, if grouping heuristics shift (due to staff turnover, policy changes, or new detection rules in months 19–24), incident boundaries may be inconsistent with the training distribution, producing systematic evaluation noise that neither model — ranker nor baseline — can navigate, and which could mask or inflate the ranker's apparent advantage.

Evidence one way or the other: Measure the stability of incident cluster sizes and composition across the three temporal windows. If average alerts-per-incident or the distribution of incident sizes shifts significantly from months 1–12 to months 19–24, the evaluation units are non-stationary, and MRR@10 is being computed over a different kind of incident in the test period. A robustness check would re-run evaluation with an alternative incident definition (e.g., time-window or IP-graph-based clustering) and compare whether the ranking advantage is preserved.

**Issue 3: MRR@10 conflates ranking quality with incident composition — and the baseline may be unfairly penalized by its inability to use listwise structure**

The claim: MRR@10 directly measures operational value and isolates ranking gains from classifier gains, because both models receive the same features.

Why it might be wrong: MRR@10 is computed per-incident. But incidents vary dramatically in size (some may have 2 alerts, some may have 200). In small incidents (2–5 alerts), even a near-random ranker will achieve MRR close to 1.0 if TP prevalence is moderate — there is almost no room for ranking to improve over classification. In large incidents (50+ alerts), ranking matters enormously. If the ranker advantage is driven entirely by large incidents, and large incidents are more common in months 19–24 (due to alert volume growth or new noisy rules), the test-period MRR advantage may reflect incident size distribution shift rather than genuine ranking ability. Separately, the baseline XGBoost classifier is trained pointwise (one alert at a time) and cannot optimize for the listwise objective directly. Framing this as 'isolating ranking-specific gains' is partly correct, but the classifier is structurally incapable of learning inter-alert relationships within an incident, even if they exist. This is not a flaw in the comparison per se — it is the whole point — but it means the comparison is also measuring 'can you exploit intra-incident correlations' and not purely 'is ranking better than scoring.' If intra-incident features are not included in the feature set, this conflation is moot; but if any feature encodes alert co-occurrence or incident-level context, the comparison is not as clean as stated.

Evidence one way or the other: Stratify MRR@10 gains by incident size quintile. If the ranker advantage is concentrated in large incidents, report this explicitly and test whether incident size is itself non-stationary across the temporal windows. Additionally, verify that no incident-level aggregate features (e.g., number of co-occurring alerts, time span of incident) are present in the feature set — if they are, the classifier baseline cannot use them in the same way the listwise ranker implicitly can.

**Summary:** The ticket-text embedding issue is the most serious because it has a specific leakage mechanism (post-resolution text content used as a triage-time feature) that could materially inflate MRR@10 for both models but more so for the ranker if ticket text correlates with high-severity TPs. The incident clustering stability issue is a genuine evaluation validity concern under temporal non-stationarity. The MRR@10 / incident-size issue is a real interpretability risk. None of these are manufactured concerns — each has a named failure mechanism and a testable remedy.
all_issues_raised: BERT embeddings fine-tuned on post-resolution ticket text may encode analyst disposition directly, creating latent label leakage through the text feature if resolved ticket content (not triage-time stub) is used.

Incident clustering defined by analyst ticket assignment may be non-stationary across temporal windows, making the evaluation unit (and thus MRR@10 denominator) inconsistent between training and test periods.
MRR@10 gains may be driven by incident size distribution rather than ranking quality, because large incidents dominate ranking difficulty and their prevalence may shift in months 19–24.
all_issues_adjudicated: BERT embeddings fine-tuned on post-resolution ticket text may encode analyst disposition directly, creating latent label leakage through the text feature if resolved ticket content (not triage-time stub) is used.

Incident clustering defined by analyst ticket assignment may be non-stationary across temporal windows, making the evaluation unit (and thus MRR@10 denominator) inconsistent between training and test periods.
MRR@10 gains may be driven by incident size distribution rather than ranking quality, because large incidents dominate ranking difficulty and their prevalence may shift in months 19–24.
